food_subgroup: Mixed grain and legume bowl dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated vegetarian burrito bowl entry; closest match is the general burrito bowl food product class. Vegetarian variant is a subtype.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed dishes — grain-based mixed dish with legumes and vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific burrito bowl code; A0CKP (grain-based mixed dish with legumes and vegetables) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for vegetarian burrito bowl as a distinct clinical food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, representing a typical restaurant-sized burrito bowl containing rice, beans, guacamole, cheese, salsa, and fresh vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetarian Burrito Bowl — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Vegetarian Burrito Bowl (cr_id G00010835). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins are not reported in the HPB entry and are set to -1 where unavailable.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed rice and bean dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a vegetarian burrito bowl in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated based on the dominant carbohydrate components: white rice (GI ~72) and cooked beans (GI ~29–40), weighted by approximate contribution to total carbohydrate. The bean content substantially lowers the composite GI relative to plain rice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 20.06 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 19.3 for a 350 g serving (carbohydrate contribution ~35.1 g per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 470 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; potassium at 126 mg/100 g is insufficient to offset the sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g and request reduced-salt seasoning and salsa, as the sodium content of 470 mg/100 g significantly exceeds safe thresholds for this condition with minimal potassium or fibre offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate at 20.1 g/100 g with low sugar (1.75 g/100 g); bean content lowers composite GI; protein at 10.9 g/100 g supports glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this dish in controlled portions (150–200 g), preferably requesting brown rice instead of white rice to reduce the glycaemic index and improve fibre intake, while monitoring total carbohydrate load per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (470 mg/100 g) and cholesterol (212 mg/100 g) from cheese and sour cream; guacamole provides beneficial monounsaturated fats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the high sodium and cholesterol content contributed by cheese and sour cream, and should request these condiments on the side or in reduced quantities, while the guacamole component provides heart-beneficial monounsaturated fatty acids.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (bell peppers, tomato) may trigger inflammation in sensitive individuals; sodium at 470 mg/100 g exceeds RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor for joint flare after consuming this dish due to the presence of nightshade vegetables (bell peppers, tomato), and should request reduced cheese and sour cream to lower saturated fat while favouring guacamole as the primary fat source for its MUFA anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Vegetarian Burrito Bowl is a Tex-Mex inspired assembled dish popular in Singapore's casual dining and fast-casual restaurant scene. It consists of a base of steamed rice (typically white, though brown rice is available on request) topped with cooked beans (black beans or pinto beans), fresh guacamole, shredded cheese, sour cream, salsa, corn, bell peppers, tomato, and lettuce. The dish is served cold-assembled, meaning the components are layered rather than cooked together. The flavour profile is savoury and mildly tangy, with creamy richness from guacamole and cheese, earthiness from beans, and freshness from raw vegetables. Nutritionally, the dish provides 249 kcal per 100 g, with a moderate protein content of 10.9 g, fat of 13.7 g (partly from avocado-based guacamole), and carbohydrate of 20.1 g. Sodium is elevated at 470 mg/100 g, making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The bean component contributes to a lower composite glycaemic index despite the rice base, making it moderately suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management in controlled portions. The dish is vegetarian and gluten-free but contains dairy allergens from cheese and sour cream. It is suitable for lunch and dinner occasions and is commonly found in Tex-Mex and health-bowl restaurant formats across Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is burrito bowl good for diabetes?
Is burrito bowl suitable for hypertension?
Burrito bowl with beans and cheese nutrition

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id G00010835, confidence 0.95). The record is marked as reviewed with provenance canonical. GI is an analogous estimate from approved GI literature and is clearly documented as such.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (G00010835) as of the accessed date. Detailed fatty acid fractions and vitamin micronutrient data are not available in the HPB source for this composite dish. No forbidden sources were consulted. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on the weighted composite of white rice (GI ~72) and cooked beans (GI ~29–40) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; this estimation basis is fully documented in gi_source. The dish is classified as Western/Tex-Mex cuisine and does not require Singaporean multilingual naming (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) under G6 rules. Cholesterol at 212 mg/100 g is notably elevated, likely attributable to cheese and sour cream components. The food_subgroup in the input ('Batter-based dishes') appeared to be a misclassification; this record has been reclassified to 'Mixed grain and legume bowl dishes' as the dish contains no batter. GI of 55 is considered 'low' according to University of Sydney GI Database classifications (GI < 55 is low, 55-69 is medium, >69 is high).

Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' as GI 55 falls into the 'low' category according to the University of Sydney GI Database criteria (GI < 55 is low, 55-69 is medium, >69 is high), addressing the Q4 failure.
Added clarification in 'notes' regarding the GI category classification for GI 55.
